ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Chilli sauce) is the closest parent; no specific sambal naraka entry exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for sambal naraka or Indonesian chili condiment at this specificity level.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 1 tablespoon (15g) of sambal naraka used as a condiment alongside a main meal; the full jar contains 220g.

nutrient_source.title: Sambal (chili sauce, Indonesian-style) — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table for Southeast Asia, adapted for bird's eye chili-dominant sambal
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service (FoodData Central) and FAO/INFOODS
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Sambal Naraka exists in any approved primary source. Values are estimated by composition analogy using USDA FoodData Central data for chili sauce / hot sauce (NDB 02055, USDA SR Legacy) and FAO/INFOODS SEA data for Indonesian sambal. Bird's eye chili-dominant sambals are high in capsaicin, moderate in fat from cooking oil, low in carbohydrate, and low in protein. Values represent a reasonable central estimate for a cooked, oil-containing chili paste at approximately 40–50% chili content with added oil and aromatics.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of chili-based condiments and hot sauces — analogous estimate based on low-carbohydrate chili sauce data
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Sambal Naraka. GI is estimated at 15 (low) by analogy with chili-based condiments and hot sauces in the University of Sydney GI Database, which consistently show low GI values due to minimal digestible carbohydrate content, high capsaicin, and high dietary fibre relative to carbohydrate. Glycemic load per 15g serving = (15 × 9.5 / 100) × 15 / 100 ≈ 0.2, confirming negligible glycemic impact.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~480 mg per 100g); typical condiment use of 15–30g per meal contributes 72–144 mg sodium
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit sambal naraka to no more than 1 teaspoon (approximately 5g) per serving to minimise sodium intake, as cumulative condiment sodium can meaningfully contribute to daily limits of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (~15) and low digestible carbohydrate content (~9.5g per 100g); capsaicin may improve insulin sensitivity
type2_diabetes.guidance: Sambal naraka has a low glycemic index and negligible glycemic load per typical serving, making it generally suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; however, portion control is advised to manage overall sodium and caloric intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate fat content (~8.5g per 100g) primarily from cooking oil; sodium content warrants monitoring; capsaicin has been associated with cardioprotective effects in some studies
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should use sambal naraka sparingly as a condiment due to its sodium and fat content from added cooking oil, while noting that capsaicin in bird's eye chillies may have modest cardioprotective properties when consumed in small amounts.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sambal Naraka (literally 'Hell Sambal' or 'Inferno Sambal' in Indonesian) is an intensely spicy Indonesian chili condiment made predominantly from bird's eye chillies (cabe rawit), shallots, garlic, cooking oil, salt, and undisclosed secret spices. It is classified as a Level 7 spice-heat product, targeting experienced chili consumers. The sambal is cooked and jarred in 220g portions. Nutritionally, per 100g it provides approximately 120 kcal, 2g protein, 8.5g fat (primarily from cooking oil), and 9.5g carbohydrate with 3.2g dietary fibre. Sodium content is notable at approximately 480 mg per 100g, making portion control important for sodium-sensitive individuals. The glycemic index is very low (estimated ~15) due to minimal digestible carbohydrate and high fibre content. Bird's eye chillies are rich in vitamin C (~76 mg per 100g) and capsaicin, which has been associated with anti-inflammatory and metabolic benefits. Sambal naraka is suitable for vegetarians, vegans, and halal diets. It is typically used as a condiment alongside rice dishes, martabak, grilled meats, or noodles. Clinically, it is low-concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to its negligible glycemic load, but moderate-concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease due to sodium and fat from cooking oil.
common_user_queries: Is sambal naraka suitable for diabetics?
What does naraka mean in Indonesian food?

confidence_reason: No exact nutritional entry for Sambal Naraka exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA, USDA). Values are estimated by composition analogy using USDA FoodData Central chili sauce data and FAO/INFOODS SEA sambal data. The food identity, cuisine classification, and ingredient profile are well-supported. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database category for chili-based condiments.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated by analogy; no exact approved-source entry for Sambal Naraka exists. Estimation basis: USDA FoodData Central NDB 02055 (hot sauce / chili sauce) and FAO/INFOODS SEA sambal composition data, adjusted for bird's eye chili dominance and added cooking oil. 2. selenium_mcg is -1 as selenium content of this specific sambal formulation is not reported in the reference sources used. 3. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 as this is a plant-based condiment containing no animal-derived ingredients; confirmed zero by formulation. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 as plant-based chili condiments contain no vitamin D; confirmed zero by formulation. 5. trans_fat_g is 0.0 as no partially hydrogenated oils are used in traditional sambal preparation; confirmed zero by formulation. 6. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as this is a fully plant-based condiment; confirmed zero by formulation. 7. GI is estimated at 15 (low) by analogy with chili-based condiments from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct measurement exists for this product. 8. The product is sold in a 220g jar at SGD 10.50 from Smile Martabak - Funan on field survey data. 9. Dietary flags (vegetarian, vegan, halal, gluten-free, dairy-free) are inferred from the ingredient profile; kosher status is unknown. 10. The 'secret spices' component introduces minor uncertainty in the nutrient estimate.
